canonical.title: Killer serves shortlist for amateur club play

parent.title: Killer serves for the amateur: a YouTube-anchored roadmap

siblings.title: Best YouTube tutorials per serve
siblings.summary: One canonical YouTube video to learn each of the three killer serves (short pendulum + no-spin twin, long fast no-spin, forehand tomahawk), one for the reverse-pendulum upgrade, plus the four channels worth subscribing to.

siblings.title: Solo and partner practice progressions
siblings.summary: How a club-level amateur should build a killer serve: 15 min/day solo with paper targets and 10-in-a-row pressure, then partner drills that walk from fixed feeder return to fully random.

siblings.title: Deception, disguise, and second-contact tricks
siblings.summary: Deep tour of the four deception levers in a table tennis serve — same-motion spin pairs, contact-point disguise, post-contact fakes, and length/direction reversal — plus what receivers actually read so you know what to hide.

sources.title: Larry Hodges · The Four Tournament Service Motions
sources.claim: Larry Hodges identifies forehand pendulum, reverse pendulum, forehand tomahawk and backhand sidespin as the four dominant tournament service motions.

sources.title: Table Tennis Teacher · Serves
sources.claim: Every player in the men's top 10 uses a pendulum-style forehand serve as their primary opener.

sources.title: Expert Table Tennis · 3 Serves Every Player Should Master
sources.claim: Chop, pendulum and reverse pendulum/shovel cover the full spin spectrum.

sources.title: Racket Insight · Learn Serving
sources.claim: Deception between heavy backspin and no-spin from an identical pendulum motion wins more points than raw spin alone.

sources.title: Tom Lodziak · The Surprising Power of a No-Spin Serve
sources.claim: Pairing a heavy-backspin pendulum with a same-motion no-spin variant traps opponents who reuse their push stroke.

sources.title: Tom Lodziak · Destroy Opponents With Long Serves
sources.claim: Amateur players underuse long serves; deep, fast serves overwhelm passive opponents.

sources.title: Tom Lodziak · How Often Should You Use Long Serves
sources.claim: Use long serves up to 80% of the time against weaker pushing/chopping opponents.

sources.title: Larry Hodges · The Free-Point Long No-Spin Serve
sources.claim: A fast no-spin serve to the playing elbow is a near-free point at amateur level when used 2–3 times per game.

sources.title: Expert Table Tennis · The Tomahawk Serve
sources.claim: Tomahawk is one of the most powerful weapons at beginner-to-intermediate level because amateurs are not grooved against its motion.

sources.title: Butterfly · Reverse Serves (Larry Hodges)
sources.claim: Reverse versions of your main serve make opponents uncomfortable simply because they have not seen them.

sources.title: Tom Lodziak · Rate Your Serve
sources.claim: Five elements of a strong service game: spin, placement, variation, deception, and recovery into the third-ball position.

sources.title: PingSunday · Third-Ball Attack
sources.claim: The canonical amateur-winning pattern is short backspin serve, opponent pushes long, server loops the third ball.

sources.title: TableTennisDaily Academy · 5 Steps to Master the Reverse Pendulum
sources.claim: Isolate the wrist square-to-table first, then add forearm and waist rotation, then change spin via contact point.

sources.title: Ferenc Horvath · 7 Most Effective Serves
sources.claim: Pendulum, reverse pendulum, backspin, no-spin, hook, tomahawk, backhand define the realistic full toolkit.

sources.title: Tom Lodziak · How to Do a Legal Serve
sources.claim: Open palm, ball stationary above the flat palm behind the end-line, near-vertical toss of at least 16 cm with no spin, ball visible throughout.
